voice.name: en-GB-SoniaNeural
voice.prosody:
Marcus Webb manages eighty-five million pounds of annual spend across a hundred and eighty suppliers. On paper it's a strategic role, but his week tells a very different story.
From Monday through to Wednesday he's copying bid data cell by cell out of eight different file formats into a scoring matrix he built three years ago, and he's opening nine browser tabs just to prepare for a single negotiation. He's even redlining an NDA on a forty-five thousand pound deal, which costs more in legal time than the risk it covers and adds two weeks to an already slow onboarding.
He's got eighty negotiations scheduled this year, and he can properly prepare for maybe fifteen of them. And as for that volume rebate buried in the twenty-twenty-two contract, he's fairly sure Centrica has never actually claimed it.
With the new model, Monday morning is just a five-minute read. The Category Watcher brief is already waiting on his screen, with two pricing alerts, a contract flagged for re-tender rather than an automatic renewal, and a supplier showing early signs of financial stress.
The RFx for a twelve million pound professional services renewal was drafted overnight, so Marcus reviews it, makes three adjustments, and it's live in Ariba by half past nine — what used to take three days now takes a single morning.
When the bids come back, every submission, whatever its format, is normalised into one evaluation matrix by the RFx Assistant, with a defensible first-pass score already in place, so Marcus goes straight to evaluation and SME review time drops by seventy to eighty percent.
And for his next negotiation, the Negotiation Buddy already has the brief ready — three years of the supplier's pricing movements, two live market benchmarks, a behavioural profile from past negotiations showing they always anchor high and move on day two, and a recommended walk-away price. Marcus reads it in fifteen minutes, and walks in better prepared than he's ever been.
And that volume rebate? The SRM Agent has been tracking it all year, and now it's claimed automatically, every quarter.
So Marcus goes from properly prepping fifteen negotiations to being genuinely ready for all eighty. The agents run the process, and Marcus drives the value.
